story.title: Liberty, Equality, Terror
story.subtitle: A Chronicle of the French Revolution, 1789–1799

theme.ambientPrompt: A period oil-painting style rendering in the manner of Jacques-Louis David and Jean-Baptiste Le Paon, tricolor cockades and torn royal banners, smoke over cobblestone Parisian streets lit by candle and cannon-flash, muted ochres and blood reds on aged parchment texture

scenes.title: A Kingdom in Debt
scenes.narration: Before the storm, there was the ledger. France, vast and populous, groaned under a monarchy that borrowed to fight wars and never learned to tax fairly. Nobles and clergy kept their exemptions while peasants and wage-earners watched bread prices climb. By the late 1780s, recession, bad harvests, and a broken exchequer had brought the whole apparatus of privilege to the edge of collapse.

citations.snippet: Economic recession from 1785, combined with bad harvests in 1787 and 1788, led to high unemployment and food prices, causing a financial and political crisis.

scenes.caption: France divided by administrative departments, mid-18th century
scenes.narration: Look upon this map of the old kingdom, stitched together from provinces of unequal privilege — Brittany here, Languedoc there, each with its own tax customs and courts. Such patchwork governance made simple reform nearly impossible, for what pleased one province enraged another, and the crown's authority frayed at every border of custom and law.

citations.snippet: French territory greatly expanded and it attempted to better integrate its provinces into an administrative whole.

scenes.quoteText: the only way to bring real order to the finances would be to revitalise the entire state by reforming everything that was defective in its constitution
scenes.attribution: Charles Alexandre de Calonne, finance minister, to the king, 1786
scenes.narration: In a private memorandum, the finance minister confessed what the court dared not say aloud: taxes could not rise, borrowing could not continue, and half-measures would not save the throne. Only sweeping reform could rescue France's finances — and reform meant confronting the very nobles Calonne served.

citations.snippet: the only way to bring real order to the finances would be to revitalise the entire state by reforming everything that was defective in its constitution

scenes.narration: Month by month the crisis deepened. Reform proposals died in the halls of privilege, the treasury bled, and public pamphlets multiplied by the hundreds. Each failed fix pushed France closer to the one instrument no king had summoned in over a century and a half: the Estates-General.

citations.snippet: on 8 August 1788, he announced that the king would summon an Estates-General to convene the following May.

scenes.title: Versailles, May 1789
scenes.narration: Twelve hundred deputies converged on Versailles that spring, clergy, nobles, and commoners summoned to a body unseen since 1614. But the old ritual soon collapsed into deadlock: would votes be counted by estate, favoring privilege, or by head, favoring the people? Six weeks of paralysis followed, and out of that stalemate a new idea was born — the nation itself, speaking through its Commons.

citations.snippet: This procedural dispute paralyzed the body for six weeks, preventing any progress on the financial crisis.

scenes.caption: Jérôme Pétion, deputy of the Third Estate
scenes.narration: Among the six hundred commoner deputies sat men like Pétion, provincial lawyers and merchants unaccustomed to royal ceremony but sharpened by years of pamphlet debate. They arrived believing they spoke for twenty-five million French men and women — and refused to be silenced by a seating chart.

citations.snippet: every male taxpayer over the age of 25 had the right to attend a primary assembly.

scenes.quoteText: Nous avons besoin du concours de nos fidèles Sujets, pour nous aider à surmonter toutes les difficultés où nous nous trouvons relativement à l'état de nos finances
scenes.attribution: Royal edict of Louis XVI, 24 January 1789
scenes.narration: The king's own summons betrayed his predicament: he needed his subjects' help to surmount the ruin of his finances. It was an admission, however courtly in phrasing, that the crown alone could no longer govern France — and it opened a door that would never again be closed.

citations.snippet: Nous avons besoin du concours de nos fidèles Sujets, pour nous aider à surmonter toutes les difficultés où nous nous trouvons relativement à l'état de nos finances

scenes.caption: Georges Danton, orator and revolutionary politician
scenes.narration: Not yet a national figure, Danton was one of countless activists organizing petitions and regional meetings during these months of ferment. His booming voice and blunt conviction would soon carry him from the clubs of Paris to the very center of revolutionary power.

citations.snippet: Activists such as Jean Joseph Mounier, Antoine Barnave and Maximilien Robespierre organised regional meetings, petitions and literature in support of these demands.

scenes.caption: Prise de la Bastille, 14 July 1789
scenes.narration: On the fourteenth of July, soldiers of the Gardes Françaises joined an armed crowd against the fortress of the Bastille. After hours of fighting that killed eighty-three attackers, its governor surrendered, was dragged to the Hôtel de Ville, and killed, his head paraded on a pike through streets suddenly alive with revolution.

citations.snippet: Its governor, Bernard-René de Launay, surrendered after several hours of fighting that cost the lives of 83 attackers.

events.label: Third Estate declares itself National Assembly
Declaration of the Rights of Man adopted

scenes.narration: In a single frantic season, the deputies who had once merely petitioned the king now abolished the feudal order outright. Tithes, seigneurial dues, and provincial privilege all fell in one nocturnal session, and by month's end France possessed a Declaration proclaiming rights once unthinkable for commoners to claim.

citations.snippet: The National Assembly abolishes the feudal system entirely.

scenes.title: Flight and War
scenes.narration: The king's own actions doomed him. His secret flight to Varennes in June 1791 shattered the fragile trust that remained, and when France went to war against Austria the following spring, defeat after defeat radicalized a public already primed for insurrection. By August 1792 the crowd would storm the Tuileries itself, and the monarchy would not survive the year.

citations.snippet: King Louis XVI's attempted flight to Varennes in June 1791 further discredited the monarchy, and military defeats after the outbreak of the French Revolutionary Wars in April 1792 led to the insurrection of 10 August 1792.

scenes.caption: Storming of the Tuileries, 10 August 1792
scenes.narration: Crowds and National Guardsmen surged into the royal palace on the tenth of August, overwhelming the Swiss Guard in brutal close combat. Within weeks the monarchy that had ruled France for centuries was formally abolished, replaced by a Republic that dated its calendar anew from this rupture.

citations.snippet: Emboldened by this, on 22 September the Convention replaced the monarchy with the French First Republic and introduced a new calendar, with 1792 becoming "Year One".

scenes.caption: The Battle of Valmy, 20 September 1792
scenes.narration: At Valmy, ragtag French conscripts and veteran gunners stood their ground against the Prussian army and, astonishingly, won. It was the Revolution's first true battlefield triumph, and it emboldened the Convention to declare the Republic just two days later.

citations.snippet: On 20 September, the French defeated the Prussians at the Battle of Valmy, in what was the first major victory by the army of France during the Revolutionary Wars.

scenes.caption: Pierre Vergniaud, leading Girondin orator
scenes.narration: Vergniaud's eloquence had helped push France toward war, hoping conflict would unify the nation behind the Revolution. Instead the Girondins he led found themselves blamed for shortages and defeats, a miscalculation that would soon cost many of them their lives.

citations.snippet: The Girondins hoped war would unite the people behind the government and provide an excuse for rising prices and food shortages. Instead, they found themselves the target of popular anger

scenes.caption: Execution of Louis XVI, Place de la Révolution, 21 January 1793
scenes.narration: Tried as Citoyen Louis Capet, the former king was condemned for conspiracy against public liberty by a narrow margin of deputies. On the twenty-first of January the sentence was carried out before a vast crowd, and the blade fell on an institution as much as a man.

citations.snippet: On 17 January 1793, Louis was sentenced to death for "conspiracy against public liberty and general safety". 361 deputies were in favour, 288 against

scenes.title: Committee and Tribunal
scenes.narration: War on every frontier and revolt in the Vendée forced the Convention to hand vast powers to a twelve-man Committee of Public Safety. Charged with defending the Republic against enemies foreign and domestic, it soon claimed near-total command over armies, courts, and ministers alike — the machinery of a Terror about to consume its own architects.

citations.snippet: It was charged with protecting the new republic against its foreign and domestic enemies, fighting the First Coalition and the Vendée revolt.

scenes.caption: The Death of Marat, by Jacques-Louis David, 1793
scenes.narration: Marat, whose fierce newspaper L'Ami du peuple had stoked revolutionary fury for years, was stabbed in his medicinal bath by Charlotte Corday, a Girondin sympathizer. David's painting transformed the radical journalist into a martyr, and his death deepened the fracture between Montagnards and Girondins beyond any hope of reconciliation.

citations.snippet: Charlotte Corday, killer of Marat.

scenes.caption: Olympe de Gouges, author of the Declaration of the Rights of Woman
scenes.narration: De Gouges insisted that if women could mount the scaffold, they should also mount the speaker's podium — that equality under the law meant nothing if half the nation was denied it. Her Declaration demanded rights in marriage, divorce, and inheritance long before such claims were fashionable, and for her Girondin sympathies she was sent to the guillotine in November 1793.

citations.snippet: Along with other Girondists, she was executed in November 1793 during the Terror.

scenes.caption: Jean-Marie Collot d'Herbois, member of the Committee of Public Safety
scenes.narration: Collot d'Herbois sat among the twelve who governed France by decree during the Terror's harshest months, men entrusted with executive power the Convention itself had surrendered. Their committee grew ever more feared as the guillotine's work multiplied across Paris and the provinces alike.

citations.snippet: In December 1793, the Convention formally conferred executive power upon the committee.

events.label: Sentenced and executed by Revolutionary Tribunal

scenes.narration: For fifteen months the machinery of revolutionary justice ran without pause. Roughly sixteen thousand men and women were condemned by the Revolutionary Tribunal, their names read aloud from vendors' programmes before crowds who came, day after day, to watch the blade fall — until in Thermidor the Terror finally turned on the men who had built it.

citations.snippet: About 16,000 people were sentenced by the Revolutionary Tribunal and executed in the Reign of Terror, which ended in July 1794 with the Thermidorian Reaction.

scenes.caption: Execution of Robespierre and Saint-Just, 28 July 1794
scenes.narration: The men who had led the Committee of Public Safety were themselves arrested and guillotined the day after their fall, Robespierre among them, his jaw bandaged from a failed suicide attempt. Vendors sold programmes of the executions, and crowds who had once cheered his speeches now watched his end with the same appetite for spectacle.

citations.snippet: On 27 July 1794, Robespierre, Saint-Just and Couthon (Mountain) were arrested and executed the following day.

scenes.title: A Fragile Republic
scenes.narration: After Robespierre's fall, power passed to a five-man Directory, a government perpetually caught between royalist resurgence and Jacobin agitation, unable to call new elections and reduced to rule by decree or by force of arms. Corruption, inflation, and an endless war wore down its legitimacy year after year, even as French armies won victories abroad.

citations.snippet: the directory was characterised by "chronic violence, ambivalent forms of justice, and repeated recourse to heavy-handed repression."

scenes.caption: Paul Barras, Director of the French Republic
scenes.narration: Barras outlasted nearly every rival, steering the Directory through royalist plots, Jacobin conspiracies, and a war that never seemed to end. His pragmatic alliances, including his patronage of a young general named Bonaparte, would ultimately hasten the very regime's demise.

citations.snippet: The removal of his conservative opponents opened the way for direct conflict between Barras and those on the left.

scenes.caption: La Révellière-Lépeaux, Director and committed Republican
scenes.narration: A firm republican among the five directors, La Révellière-Lépeaux worked to keep the fragile regime from collapsing under royalist pressure. He was part of the majority that annulled the 1797 elections rather than see a restoration undo everything the Revolution had achieved.

citations.snippet: Prior to 1797, three of the five directors were firmly Republican; Barras, Révellière-Lépeaux and Jean-François Rewbell

events.label: Coup of 18 Brumaire ends the Directory

scenes.narration: Through five turbulent years the Directory lurched from crisis to coup, unable to reconcile the Revolution's radical promises with the need for order. Its final act came on 18 Brumaire, when Napoleon Bonaparte, the soldier who had once defended the Convention with cannon fire, swept the regime aside to install himself as First Consul.

citations.snippet: Its instability ended in 1799 with the coup of 18 Brumaire and the establishment of the Consulate, with Napoleon Bonaparte as First Consul.

scenes.caption: Cambacérès, jurist of the revolutionary era
scenes.narration: A survivor of every revolutionary regime from the Convention onward, Cambacérès embodied the legal continuity that outlasted the turmoil — the codification of equality before the law that no subsequent government, however conservative, dared fully undo. Through men like him, the Revolution's legal legacy passed intact into the new century.

citations.snippet: there were no efforts by the Bourbons, Vichy or any other government to restore the privileges that had been stripped away from the nobility in 1789. France permanently became a society of equals under the law.

scenes.quoteText: Revolution became a tradition, and republicanism an enduring option.
scenes.attribution: Historian Paul Hanson, on the legacy of the Revolution
scenes.narration: Even after empire and restoration swept away many revolutionary gains, the memory of participatory politics endured in French life. What began as a fiscal crisis at Versailles had, within a single decade, remade the very idea of what a nation could be — and that idea would outlive every regime that tried to bury it.

citations.snippet: According to Paul Hanson, "Revolution became a tradition, and republicanism an enduring option."
